coredata.dc:title: Nonconventional biocompatible macromolecular AEEgens for sensitive detections and removals of Cu(II) and Fe(III): N and/ or O donor(s) selective coordinations of metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Dutta, Arnab
Mahapatra, Manas
Mitra, Madhushree
Banerjee, Akash
Ghosh, Narendra Nath
Chattopadhyay, Pijush Kanti
Maiti, Dilip K.
Singha, Nayan Ranjan
coredata.dc:description: For sensitive detections and repetitive exclusions of excess Cu(II)/ Fe(III) from hazardous effluents and high-contrast time dependent Madin-Darby canine kidney cell imaging by photobleaching resistant durable non-aromatic biodegradable biocompatible fluorogens, two non-conventional purely aliphatic macromolecular luminogens, i.e., butyl-2-propenoate-co-butyl 3-acrylamidopropanoate-co-prop-2-enamide (i.e., BP-co-BAP-co-PE, M1) and ethylene glycol methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol 3-acrylamido-2-methylpropanoate-co-prop-2-enamide (i.e., EGM-co-EGAMP-co-PE, M2), were prepared through the in situ anchoring of third acrylamido-ester comonomers during polymerization of two monomers and the optimization of ex situ added monomer ratios. Structures of as-synthesized nonconventional luminogens, in situ anchored BAP/ EGAMP, and aggregation-enhanced emissions/ AEEs were understood by exploring microstructures through XPS, 1H/ 13C NMR, FTIR, UV–vis, TGA, DSC, HRTEM, DLS, fluorescence imaging, fluorescence lifetime, and network parameters. The emission properties, fluorescence quenching, and exclusions of Cu(II) and Fe(III) were investigated in organic solvents and pure water, inferred through computational studies of M1/ M2 and Cu(II)-M1/ Fe(III)-M2 complexes. Herein, the strong coordination of O/ N donor(s), i.e., –CO N H2, –CO N H–, and –C O OCH2–, with Cu(II) and O donors, i.e., –C O OCH2– and –C O NH–, with Fe(III) in Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes, respectively, were examined through XPS, FTIR, TG, and SEM analyses of those complexes; pseudosecond order model fitting; and activation energies of adsorption. The limits of detection of M1 and M2 were 1.69 × 10–7 and 1.88 × 10–7 M, respectively. The adsorption capacity maxima of M1 and M2 under optimum conditions were 130.64 and 100.04 mg g−1, respectively, within 5–50 ppm and at 303 K for 0.01 g M1/ M2.

XPS spectra of (a/ e) N1s, (b/ f) O1s, and (c/ g) C1s for Cu(II)-M1/ Fe(III)-M2, (d) Cu2p for Cu(II)-M1, and (h) Fe2p for Fe(III)-M2; TG analyses of (i) M1/ Cu(II)-M1 and (j) M2/ Fe(III)-M2; SEM microphotographs of (k) Cu(II)-M1 and (l) Fe(III)-M2 at 10 kX magnification; and EDX spectra of (m) Cu(II)-M1 and (n) Fe(III)-M2.
Adsorption-desorption cycles of (a) Cu(II)-M1 and (b) Fe(III)-M2 and DLS of (c) Cu(II)-M1 and (d) Fe(III)-M2.

Concentration dependent cell viabilities of (a/ b) M1/ M2 treated MDCK cells, fluorescence imaging of MDCK cells treated with M1/ M2 after (c/ d) 20, (e/ f) 40, and (g/ h) 80 min of incubation through FITC (i.e., 520 nm) channel, confocal images of (j) M1- and (k) M2-treated MDCK cells, and variation in fluorescence intensities with time in selected zones of (i) M1- and (l) M2-treated MDCK cells.
Synthesis of green light-emitting M1/ M2 for on-off sensing of Cu(II) and Fe(III).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For sensitive detections and repetitive exclusions of excess Cu(II)/ Fe(III) from hazardous effluents and high-contrast time dependent Madin-Darby canine kidney cell imaging by photobleaching resistant durable non-aromatic biodegradable biocompatible fluorogens, two non-conventional purely aliphatic macromolecular luminogens, i.e., butyl-2-propenoate-co-butyl 3-acrylamidopropanoate-co-prop-2-enamide (i.e., BP-co-BAP-co-PE, M1) and ethylene glycol methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol 3-acrylamido-2-methylpropanoate-co-prop-2-enamide (i.e., EGM-co-EGAMP-co-PE, M2), were prepared through the in situ anchoring of third acrylamido-ester comonomers during polymerization of two monomers and the optimization of ex situ added monomer ratios. Structures of as-synthesized nonconventional luminogens, in situ anchored BAP/ EGAMP, and aggregation-enhanced emissions/ AEEs were understood by exploring microstructures through XPS, 1H/ 13C NMR, FTIR, UV–vis, TGA, DSC, HRTEM, DLS, fluorescence imaging, fluorescence lifetime, and network parameters. The emission properties, fluorescence quenching, and exclusions of Cu(II) and Fe(III) were investigated in organic solvents and pure water, inferred through computational studies of M1/ M2 and Cu(II)-M1/ Fe(III)-M2 complexes. Herein, the strong coordination of O/ N donor(s), i.e., –CO N H2, –CO N H–, and –C O OCH2–, with Cu(II) and O donors, i.e., –C O OCH2– and –C O NH–, with Fe(III) in Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes, respectively, were examined through XPS, FTIR, TG, and SEM analyses of those complexes; pseudosecond order model fitting; and activation energies of adsorption. The limits of detection of M1 and M2 were 1.69 × 10–7 and 1.88 × 10–7 M, respectively. The adsorption capacity maxima of M1 and M2 under optimum conditions were 130.64 and 100.04 mg g−1, respectively, within 5–50 ppm and at 303 K for 0.01 g M1/ M2.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, nonaromatic macromolecular aggregation enhanced emission luminogens (AEEgens) are presenting substantial attention to the researchers for cell imaging [1], biosensing [2], data security [3], and drug delivery [4]. In general, compared to purely aliphatic polymers, aromatic macromolecules have poor biodegradability, inferior UV resistance, and higher susceptibility towards photodegradation. To overcome such difficulties, few nonaromatic polymers have recently been introduced as better alternatives of traditional aromatic fluorescent materials [5]. Indeed, several limitations of conventional aromatic macromolecular probes are circumvented by developing nonaromatic biocompatible macromolecular AEEgens [6]. The purely aliphatic macromolecular sensors are stable and durable due to the minimum susceptibility towards photobleaching [5]. However, purely aliphatic biodegradable multifunctional macromolecular AEEgens comprising biocompatible in situ protruded acrylamido-ester monomer have been very rarely reported [5,7].

The emissions from polymers are improved by the N-branching associated elevated rigidity and restricted interior mobility [8–11]. Moreover, the strategic selection of monomer(s) and polymerisation technique can endow long-term durability and elevated aggregation-enhanced emission (AEE) of polymers. Because of the enhanced UV resistance and lesser susceptibility towards photodegradation, an aliphatic monomer should be a better choice than an aromatic monomer. Here, PE has been chosen as one of the aliphatic monomers for synthesizing M1/ M2, as side chain of PEM (primary amide) is expected to function as an in situ comonomer attachment site via NC coupling [12]. Moreover, the long hydrophobic chain of BP enhances the rigidity in M1. Additionally, enhanced rigidity in M2 can be conferred by α-methyl branches and hydrogen bondings ability of OH functionalities contributed by EGM [13]. Thus, relatively hydrophobic M2 should possess higher aggregating tendency due to α-methyl functionality at the backbone, ester side chain, and O–H functionalities. Moreover, in contrast to the surfactant assisted conventional emulsion polymerization, surfactant free facile polymerization of hydrophobic monomer(s) in aqueous solutions is expected to encourage aggregation and AEEs via hydrophobic interactions and inter-/ intra-molecular hydrogen bonding. Moreover, because of partial double bond character, in situ attached secondary amides should elevate rigidity and fluorescence capabilities of AEEgens. Thus, nonaromatic fluorescent macromolecular AEEgens have been strategically synthesized by CN and CC coupled surfactant free solution polymerization (Scheme 1 ) of hydrophobic aliphatic monomer(s) in aqueous solutions in which third acrylamido-ester comonomers have been anchored in situ.

Industrial effluents usually contain alarmingly higher level of chemically hazardous species including Fe(III) and Cu(II). Though iron has pivotal roles in chlorophyll biosynthesis, photosynthesis, oxygen uptake, respiration, and metabolism in plants, abnormal concentration of iron inflicts various disorders including endotoxemia, hepatic cirrhosis, and hereditary hemochromatosis in human beings [14]. Similarly, up to a certain limiting concentration, Cu(II) functions as micronutrient. Moreover, several Cu(II)-compounds act as potent antimicrobial agents preventing foliar diseases caused by plant pathogenic bacteria, fungi, and oomycetes. However, an excessive Cu(II) acts as plant stressor inflicting damage during flushing or blooming and germination, along with depreciating fruit values and soil fertility [15]. The hepatitis, mental detritions, tremors, and azotaemia congenital diseases are the undesirable consequences of excess Cu(II) in human-body [13]. Moreover, prolonged Cu(II) deficiency causes anemia, growth-/ mental-retardation, and hypothermia. Both Fe(III) and Cu(II) have pronounced paramagnetic quenching effects on fluorogens [16–19]. Though few on-off macromolecular luminogens have been developed as Cu(II) and Fe(III) sensors, these probes are not suitable for diverse applications [20–30].

Recently, few fluorescent polymeric probes have been reported as Cu(II) and Fe(III) sensors [31–36]. For instance, chromofluorogenic N,N-dimethylaminoethylaminoanthrone based chemosensor has been prepared for selective Cu(II) sensing in which N-/ O-donor(s), i.e., −NMe2, −NH−, and −CONH−, selectively bind with Cu(II) [31]. Moreover, anthraquinone coupled benzothiazole based receptor has been synthesiszed for selective Cu(II) sensing, wherein −CONH−, −O−, and >C=O of receptor bind selectively with Cu(II) [32]. Likewise, rhodamine-ethynylferrocene conjugate has been prepared for multichannel detection of Cu(II) in the presence of competing ions. Here, −CONH− and −CONHNH2 of rhodamine-ethynylferrocene conjugate form stable complex with Cu(II) [33]. In addition, coumarin appended pseudo crown receptor has been prepared for selective recognition of Fe(III) in which −OCH2CH2 OCH2CH2 OCH2CH2 OCH2CH2 O−, −O−, and −OCH3 bind selectively with Fe(III) [34]. Moreover, hydroxamic acid based polyethers of AB (di-block) and ABA (tri-block) polymers have been prepared for efficient Fe(III) binding [35]. Here, hydroxamic acid side chains of polyether backbone attached through O-donors, i.e., −CO−NH−OH, resulted in tris-, bis-, or mono(hydroxamato)iron(III) complex depending on the amount of Fe(III) [35]. Hyperbranched π-conjugated polymers comprising 1,3-butadiene and carboxylic ester units in main chain and side chain, respectively, have been synthesized by metal-free catalytic polymerization of diethyl 4,4′-(5-formyl-1,3-phenylene)(2E,2′E)-bis(but-2-enoate) and dimethyl 4,4′-(5-formyl-1,3-phenylene)(2E,2′E)-bis(but-2-enoate) for selective Fe(III) sensing. Herein, O-donors, i.e., −COOR and –COOR, bind preferentially with Fe(III) [36]. Thus, M1 and M2 composed of N- and O-donors, such as −CONH2, −CONH−, −CH2OH, and −COOCH2−, are expected to form stronger Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes through N-/ O- and O-donors, respectively.

Earlier, nanoparticles [37], conjugated polymers [38], and protein based polymers [39] employed for cell imaging present poor biocompatibility, durability, and reusability alongside high susceptibility towards photobleaching [40]. Moreover, in cell imaging, the conventional aromatic AEEgens are employed at low concentration to circumvent the aggregation driven fluorescence quenching [41]. Recently, the nonaromatic intrinsically fluorescent macromolecular AEEgens are drawing attractive attention due to their ability to avoid several limitations related to the conventional fluorescent probes and aromatic sensors [42]. The AEEgens combining several advantages in aggregated states, such as high emission efficiency [43,44], strong photostability [45], high signal-to-noise ratio [46], large Stokes’ shift [47], and low background noise in dilute solution [48], are suitable for variable sensing applications. Accordingly, fluorescent macromolecular AEEgens comprising biocompatible acrylamido-ester, ester, and amide functionalities are encouraged in imaging, cellular adhesion, and medical applications. Thus, M1 and M2 pre-designed with biocompatible amides and hydrophobic functionalities should be photobleaching resistant, durable, and facilitate cell interaction in high-contrast cell imaging and AEEs. Moreover, the intensity can be amplified using high concentrations of fluorescent polymers [49].

Therefore, two environment-friendly, biodegradable, and biocompatible nonaromatic macromolecular AEEgens comprising amide and variable ester functionalities and presenting aggregating tendencies have been prepared by attaching acrylamido-ester monomers in situ, using two ex situ non-fluorescent purely aliphatic monomers for applications in sensitive and selective Cu(II) and Fe(III) sensing; high-performance repetitive exclusions of Cu(II) and Fe(III); cell imaging; and invisible inks. The photophysical characteristics, intrinsic fluorescence and turn-off mechanisms, binding modes, nature of interactions of Cu(II)/ Fe(III) with N or O donors of M1 and M2 in complexes, and associated Cu(II)/ Fe(III) adsorption have been investigated via computational analyses of M1 and M2 together with Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes.

The fluorescence enhancement (I/I 0) maxima as well as higher ρ c were presented by the optimum M1 and M2. Up to 22 vol% water additions in DMSO, I/I 0 = 1.25, 1.30, 1.55, and 1.53 were recorded for 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, and 1:20 BP/PE in M1, respectively, related to the size variations in aggregated states (Fig. 1 a−d). Likewise, 22 vol% water addition in DMSO resulted in I/I 0 = 2.10, 2.00, 1.85, and 1.78 for 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, and 1:40 EGM/PE in M2, respectively (Fig. 1e−h). Thus, optimum M1 (i.e., I/I 0 = 1.55 at BP/PE = 1:10) and M2 (i.e., I/I 0 = 2.10 at EGM/PE = 1:5) were selected for photophysical, sensing, adsorption, and related studies.

The fluorescent AEEgens, such as butyl-2-propenoate-co-butyl 3-acrylamidopropanoate-co-prop-2-enamide (i.e., BP-co-BAP-co-PE, M1) and ethylene glycol methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol 3-acrylamido-2-methylpropanoate-co-prop-2-enamide (i.e., EGM-co-EGAMP-co-PE, M2), were characterized using 1H/ 13C NMR, UV–vis spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy/ XPS, Fourier transform infrared/ FTIR spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy/ HRTEM, and thermogravimetric/ TG analysis. The disappearance of two carbon and vinylic proton peaks within 125.53–135.82 and 5.57–6.56 ppm (Fig. S1a,b and 2a,b) confirmed saturated structures of M1 and M2 formed by the facile polymerization of two monomers, such as BP + PE and EGM + PE. The saturations were further confirmed by −CH2−/ >CH− backbone peaks within 0.83–1.58/ 1.89–2.77 ppm and 29.41−43.17 ppm in NMR spectra (Figs. 2 a,b and S1a,b). In M1 and M2, BP and EGM were confirmed from −O C H2−/ > C =O peaks at 65.65/ 180.92 and 62.14/ 181.09 ppm, supported by >CO str. of −COOCH2– at 1736 and 1729 cm−1, respectively (Table S1 and Fig. S2). Moreover, in M1/ M2, PE was confirmed by CN str. of −CONH2 (i.e., amide-III) and >CO str. of −CONH2 (i.e., amide-I) at 1415 and 1667/ 1655 cm−1, respectively (Table S1 and Fig. S2). Most importantly, both in M1 and M2, the new peak at 3.49 confirmed −CONH−C H
2−, i.e., acrylamido moieties, of in situ anchored acrylamido-ester monomers, i.e., BAP and EGAMP, substantiated from the peaks at 42.95 and 43.17 ppm in 13C NMR of M1 and M2, respectively [12,51–53]. The in situ conversions from primary amides to secondary amides enhanced the rigidity of M1/ M2, facilitating fluorescence enhancement. Moreover, the in situ anchored secondary amides and hydrogen bonded water clusters were substantiated by O1s peaks (satellite) at 540.49 and 541.47 eV in M1 and M2, respectively, originated by π–π* transition in –C O NH−CH2– moieties.

In M1 and M2, in situ anchored acrylamido moieties were further confirmed from O1s, N1s, and C1s binding energies (BEs) (Fig. 3 ). The N1s BEs at 399.62/ 399.91 and 398.75/ 399.42 eV in M1 and M2, respectively, confirmed coexistences of −CO N H2 and −CO N H‒ functionalities (Fig. 3a,d). In M1 and M2, O1s BEs at 532.29 and 532.03 eV, respectively, confirmed the coexistences of –C O NH2 and –C O NH– functionalities suitable for elevating rigidity and AEEs (Fig. 3b,e).

In M1 and M2, > C H‒/ ‒ C H2‒/ ‒ C H3, C ‒N/ C ‒O, and > C =O functionalities were confirmed from C1s BEs within 284.89–285.05, 286.21–286.99, and 288.25–288.69 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c,f). The TG thermograms of M1 and M2 followed similar trends. Indeed, M2 presented marginally elevated stability within 30–220 oC due to higher hydrogen bonding contributed by OH functionalities. Above 220 oC, the degradations of M1 and M2 were correlated to conversions of primary amides to imides by the loss of NH3. The astricted cyclization of in situ anchored secondary amides to imides (Fig. 3g) resulted in prominent shoulders within 300–350 oC in the thermograms of M1 and M2. Here, almost negligible residues of M1 and M2 were related to the retentions of low fractions thermoresistant inorganic components in hydrophobic matrices. The lower critical solution temperatures (LCSTs) of M1 and M2 at 88.31 and 80.25 oC (Fig. 3h), respectively, were ascribed to variable coil-to-globule transitions and rigidity controlled by n–π* interaction associated aggregations and hydrogen bonding.

The elevated emission in solid state (lower panel of Scheme 1) was correlated to intra-/ inter-chain interactions, conformational rigidity, and chain entanglement assisted clustering of the chromophores, facilitating the closer approach and through space conjugation via n–π* interactions. Accordingly, the energy differences between S0 and S1 lowered significantly, leading to substantial red shifted emission in M1 and M2. The excitation-dependent emissions of M1 and M2 were realized via shifting of emission spectra towards higher λ em at higher wavelength of excitations, i.e., λex = 280, 300, 320, 360, and 400 nm (Fig. S3) [49]. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra recorded in DMSO at 300 nm/ λex (Fig. S4a,b) confirmed non-fluorescent nature of two ex situ monomers. Importantly, M1 and M2 synthesized employing higher initiator doses converted primary amides to secondary amides through NC coupling, confirmed from XPS, NMR, and FTIR analyses. The roles of in situ anchored relatively rigid acrylamido-ester monomers in the intrinsically fluorescent M1 and M2 were explored by the DFT and NTO analyses. The fluorescence intensities of M1 and M2 in CHCl3 at 300 nm/ λex (Fig. S5a,b) reduced via gradual dilution. The emissions of aggregates formed due to water addition into water + DMSO solution were explored at 300 nm/ λex. Importantly, AEEs (Fig. 4 a–d) assisted the enhancement of fluorescence intensities at higher water volume fraction in M1/ M2 + DMSO solution (Fig. 4a,b) [54].

The higher water volume amount decreased solubilities of M1 and M2 in water + DMSO solutions, as M1 and M2 were less soluble in water (Fig. 4a,b). The AEE maxima in M1 and M2 were noted at 22 and 25 % (v/v) water/DMSO, respectively. The fluorescence enhancement/ (I/I 0) of M1 and M2 increased to 1.55 and 2.10, respectively, in water + DMSO solutions by 22 vol% water additions in DMSO (Fig. 4a,b) due to the formations of aggregates having variable sizes. Thus, the aggregation restrained self-quenching/ relaxation, and increased fluorescence intensities. It is noticeable that above the intensity maxima, the intensities of emissions reduced by any further water additions. The aggregates in water + DMSO solutions were confirmed by HRTEM images (Fig. 4c,d), supported via dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement (Fig. 4e,f) envisaging enhanced hydrodynamic diameters/ D h, of M1 and M2 from 146 and 129 nm to 317 and 269 nm, respectively (Fig. 4e,f). The variable additions of water formed aggregates of variable sizes based on hydrophilic-to-hydrophobic ratios, sizes of M1 and M2, and types of solution phase, supported from higher aggregates of M1 and M2 in relatively polar solvents, i.e., DMSO and CH3OH (Figs. 4e,f and S6). Both emission and absorption spectra of M1 and M2 were recorded in polar solvents, from which the peak position alterations were correlated to different polarities of solutions, presenting polar ground/ excited states of M1 and M2. The absorption peaks in M1 and M2 appeared within 202–282 nm by π–π* transition, along with weaker n–π* shoulders at higher wavelengths (Fig. S7a,b). In polar solvents, the bathochromic shifts (Fig. S8a,b) in emission spectra of M1 and M2 were ascribed to lesser energy differences among excited and ground states, as the excited states showed higher stabilities in polar medium. Importantly, fluorescence maxima emerged at larger wavelengths in DMSO than that of more polar CH3OH. Instead, solute-solvent hydrogen bonding interactions presented a pivotal role in intrinsic emissions of M1 and M2 in the polar protic solvent. The greater stabilities of excited states of M1 and M2 were substantiated by the change in solvent from nonpolar to polar aprotic. The decrease in emissions from DMSO to CH3OH was attributed to the creation of excessive intermolecular hydrogen bonds in M1 and M2. The hydrogen bonding interactions associated in situ formed conformations were substantiated through double-hump fluorescence spectra of M1 and M2 [55] and exponential fitting of emission decay curves (Fig. 4g,h). The lifetimes of AEEgens varied within 1.01–1.56 ns.

In M1 and M2, C=O lengths of BAP/ EGAMP increased from 1.24 Å in S0 to 1.28 Å in S1 (Fig. 5 a,b). Moreover, C−N lengths of BAP/ EGAMP altered from 1.36 Å in S0 to 1.54/ 1.31 Å in S1. In M2, EGM structure altered substantially showing increased C–O/ C=O lengths from 1.35/ 1.22 Å in S0 to 1.50/ 1.30 Å in S1.

In S0, the planar amide and ester carbonyl centres distorted in S1 (Fig. 5a,b). The energies of frontier molecular orbitals/ FMOs and HOMO–LUMO energy gaps/ HL were presented in Table S2−S3 and Fig. 5 and S9−S12, respectively. In DMSO medium, the decrease in HL range of M1/ M2 from 6.55/ 6.62 eV in S0 to 0.86/ 2.01 eV in S1 confirmed facilitated electronic transitions (Fig. 5c). Indeed, oscillator strengths/ electronic excitations of low energy excited states comprising larger configuration interaction/ CI coefficients were incorporated in Table S4−S6. In M1 and M2, n–π* transitions from nitrogen/ oxygen in BAP + PE and EGAMP to π* of BAP and EGAMP presented oscillator strengths/ f = 0.013 and 0.017, respectively. In M1 and M2, the low-lying absorption bands within 235−254 nm in DMSO appeared in the proximity of experimentally obtained bands within 281–282 nm (Fig. S7), originated mainly via H−2→L/ S0→S1 and H−6→L/ S0→S1 transitions, respectively. Moreover, in DMSO, UV–vis bands of M1 and M2 shifted from 235 and 230 nm to 259 and 263 nm in the twisted aggregates, respectively (Fig. S13), wherein the different extent of twistings in partially distorted aggregates were confirmed by Δλ = 24 and 33 nm in M1 and M2, respectively. In M1 and M2, the stronger charge transfer caused emissions at 381 nm (Table S7), confirmed by smaller wave function overlap/ σ he, i.e., 0.14 and 0.13, and larger separation/ Δr, i.e., 4.87 and 4.10 Å (Table S8) [56,57]. In M1 and M2, HONTOs/ LUNTOs localized mostly on PE + BAP/ BAP and PE + EGAMP/ EGAMP, respectively (Fig. 5).

The interactions of M1 and M2 with K+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Sb3+, and Bi3+ were compared in 3:1 (v/v) DMSO/water at excitation wavelength/ λ ex = 300 nm, concentration of metal ions = 1.0 × 10−2 [M], and pH = 7.0 by employing 5/ 10 nm slit for the source/ detector. Herein, the emission spectra of Cu(II) and Fe(III) envisaged significant quenching of fluorescence (Fig. 6 a,b) by forming stable Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes. In fluorescence titration, the intensities of emission spectra decreased with increasing Cu(II) and Fe(III) concentrations in solutions of M1 and M2 in water + DMSO, respectively (Fig. 6c,d). The quenching efficiencies (η) at 432 and 435 nm were 96.05 and 95.08 % for M1 and M2 by adding 63.67 × 10−5 [M] Cu(II) and 63.67 × 10−5 [M] Fe(III), respectively. For remaining metal ions, the maximum η values were 42.94 and 44.59 % for M1 and M2, respectively. The titration plots presented linear decreasing trend in emission intensities (Fig. 6c,d). The limits of detection (LODs)/ competitive experiments were conducted at lower concentrations for envisaging highly sensitive turn-off Cu(II) and Fe(III) sensors. The LODs, i.e., 1.69 × 10−7 and 1.88 × 10−7 [M] (S/N = 3) for M1 and M2, respectively, were much lower compared to reported values (Fig. 7 a,b and Table 2 ) [13,24,36,58–64]. Moreover, in pure water, the interactions of M1 and M2 with Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively, were investigated at excitation wavelength (λ ex) =300 nm, metal ions within 0−53.03 × 10−5 [M], and pH = 7.0 by employing 5/ 10 nm slit for the source/ detector. Here, the intensities of emission spectra decreased with increasing Cu(II) and Fe(III) concentrations in aqueous solutions of M1 and M2, respectively (Fig. S14a,b). The maximumη calculated at 405 nm by the addition of 53.03 × 10−5 [M] Cu(II)/ Fe(III) were 92.15 and 89.50 % for M1 and M2, respectively (Fig. S14c,d). The K sv of 1.57 × 103 and 1.42 × 103 M−1 were calculated for M1 and M2, respectively, within 0−1.96 × 10−5 and 0−2.34 × 10−5 [M] of Cu(II) and Fe(III) (Fig. S15a,b). The LODs were 2.66 × 10−6 and 3.15 × 10−6 [M] (S/N = 3) for M1 and M2, respectively (Fig. S15c,d) [60,64].

Moreover, the competitive experiments were conducted at 50 mM to envisage the selectivities of M1 and M2 towards Cu(II) and Fe(III), respectively. The effect of emission quenching in Cu(II)/ Fe(III) + solution of other metal ion was almost negligible than those of individual Cu(II) and Fe(III) solutions (Fig. S16a,b). The maximum of fluorescence quenching was noted in Cu(II) + Fe(III) binary solution, as Fe(III) and Cu(II) competed for binding with M1/ M2. Notably, no other metal ion interfered the binding of M1 and M2 with Cu(II) and Fe(III), respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence titration was carried out using 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3 Cu(II)/Fe(III), from which the intensity of emission maxima of M1 and M2 decreased gradually with increasing Cu(II) and Fe(III) concentrations, respectively (Fig. S17a−f). In M1, quenching efficiencies = 85.72, 80.01, and 64.49 % were recorded via additions of 63.67 × 10−5 [M] 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3 Cu(II)/Fe(III) solutions, respectively (Fig. S17a−c). Likewise, in M2, quenching efficiencies = 85.19, 79.40, and 72.64 % were noted via addition of 63.67 × 10−5 M 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3 Fe(III)/Cu(II) solutions, respectively (Fig. S17d−f). Thus, dual ion sensing experiments showed suitability of M1 and M2 as Cu(II) and Fe(III) sensors, respectively. The fluorescence quenching accompanied by linear alterations within 0–3.1 × 10−4 [M] Cu(II) and 0–2.7 × 10−4 M Fe(III) for M1 and M2, respectively (Fig. 7c–f). Though the initial linear response presented either dynamic or static quenching [65], the greater quenching at higher concentrations of Fe(III) and Cu(II) substantiated either dynamic + static or static quenching (Fig. 7e,f) [66]. However, only static quenching mechanism was confirmed by unchanged fluorescence lifetimes of M1 and M2 upon additions of Cu(II) and Fe(III), respectively (Fig. 6e,f). In this regard, Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes at ground states were identified from increasing intensities of peaks at 280/ 988 and 271/ 321/ 992 nm in absorption spectra due to gradual additions of Cu(II) and Fe(III), respectively (Fig. S18a,b). Moreover, the Stern-Volmer quenching constants/ KSV of M1 and M2 were estimated as 3.04 × 103 and 2.10 × 103 [M]−1, respectively (Fig. 7e,f). The complexation at S0 among M1/ M2 and Cu(II)/ Fe(III) made both AEEgens to be suitable further for exclusions from more concentrated solutions. The simulated UV–vis bands shifted from 235 and 254 nm in M1 and M2 to 415 and 447 nm in Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes, respectively, altered energy levels and major transition characteristics in M1/ M2 (Table S9−S12). In Cu(II)-M1, the highest f = 0.110 transitions were H−16(β)→LUMO(β) and H−14(β)→LUMO(β) in which the LOMOs located mostly on Cu(II) (Table S9–S10 and Fig. S19).

Likewise, in Fe(III)-M2, the highest oscillator strength f = 0.012 transitions were H−19(β)→LUMO(β) and H−15(β)→LUMO(β) in which the LOMOs located mainly on Fe(III) (Fig. S20). The strong paramagnetic quenching of Cu(II) and Fe(III) ions, reduction of hydrogen bonding, and pre-existed amide/ ester n–π* through space coordinative conjugation endowed substantial fluorescence quenching (Table S9–S10).

The pHPZC of M1 and M2 were 6.57 and 6.44, respectively (Fig. S21a). In water medium, Cu(II)/ Fe(III) exists as free ion within pHi = 3.0–7.0. Above pHi = 7.0, Cu(II)/ Fe(III) forms [M(OH)n]n+ complexes [67,68]. Thus, the maximum q max were noted at pHi = 7.0 (Fig. S21b). The optimum doses were confirmed from adsorption experiments at 40 mg L–1, pHi = 7.0, and 298 K, using M1/ M2 within 0.002–0.020 g, where q e decreased by the increase in M1/ M2 amount. Since the optimum results were obtained at 0.01 g (Fig. S21c,d), adsorption experiments were carried out at optimum dose of M1/ M2 = 0.01 g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption isotherm, kinetics, and thermodynamics studies
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical measurements inferred the maximum binding affinities of Cu(II) and Fe(III) with M1 and M2, respectively. Thus, M1 and M2 were suitable for removals of Cu(II)/ Fe(III) from concentrated solutions, realized from ground state coordination of Cu(II) and Fe(III) with M1 and M2, respectively. The adsorption data at equilibrium were fitted to Langmuir, BET, and Freundlich isotherms [69–71], of which Langmuir adsorption isotherm (Fig. 8 a,b) was confirmed to fit the best (Table S13–S14)[72–75]. As expected, the higher removal efficiencies even in the presence of hydrophobic functionalities were confirmed by q max values, i.e., 130.64 and 100.04 mg g–1 for M1 and M2, respectively.

The chemical bond formations of Cu(II) and Fe(III) with M1 and M2, respectively, were confirmed further by fitting parameters of pseudosecond order kinetics models (Fig. 8c,d) [76,77] (Table S14) and activation energy/ E a of adsorption, i.e., 51.06 and 26.21 kJ mol–1 for M1 and M2, respectively (Fig. S22) [78,79]. The exothermic nature was realized from –ΔH 0 (Table 3 ) [80]. Moreover, –ΔG 0 and +ΔS 0 substantiated the favorable binding of Cu(II) and Fe(III) with M1 and M2, respectively (Fig. 8e,f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Cu(II) and Fe(III) complexes
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption energies for the bindings of Cu(II)/ Fe(III) with –C O OCH2–/ −CO N H–/ −CO N H2 in M1 and –C O OCH2–/ –C O NH– in M2 were 6.84 and 5.40 eV. Thus, theoretically computed higher energies of adsorption confirmed further the chemical nature of adsorption relating to q max = 130.64 and 100.04 mg g–1 for M1 and M2, respectively (Table S14). Because of the Cu(II) coordination, CO/ CO, CO/ CN/ NC, and CN/ CO bond lengths of −COOCH2–, −CONH−CH2–, and −CONH2 altered from 1.22/ 1.34, 1.24/ 1.36/ 1.46, and 1.35/ 1.24 Å in M1 to 1.26/ 1.29, 1.20/ 1.48/ 1.51, and 1.46/ 1.20 Å in Cu(II)-M1, respectively (Fig. 9 a). Similarly, CO/ CO and CN/ CO bond lengths of −COOCH2– and −CONH– altered from 1.32/ 1.24 and 1.32/ 1.28 Å in M2 to 1.35/ 1.22 and 1.36/ 1.24 Å in Fe(III)-M2, respectively (Fig. 9b). Moreover, charge distribution and hydrogen bonding altered via Cu(II)/ Fe(III) coordination with M1/ M2 (Fig. 9). Notably, Cu(II)-M1/ Fe(III)-M2 complexes were investigated further by XPS, FTIR, and TG analyses (Table S15–S16 and Figs. 10 and S23) to substantiate the nature of interaction(s) and their effects on emissions of M1 and M2 via Cu(II) and Fe(III) addition, respectively. Here, stronger coordination of N-donors, i.e., −CO N H2 and −CO N H–, with Cu(II) was confirmed by substantial shift of N1s BEs from 398.75 and 399.62 eV in M1 to 399.42 and 400.34 eV in Cu(II)-M1 complex, respectively (Fig. 3a and 10 a).

Similar to N-donors, the stronger coordination of O-donor, i.e., –C O OCH2–, with Cu(II) was confirmed via elevated O1s BEs from 532.29 eV in M1 to 532.85 eV in Cu(II)-M1 complex (Figs. 3b and 10 b). Such N and O donors selective coordination of Cu(II) were substantiated via decrease in Cu2p3/2/ Cu2p1/2 BEs from 935.50/ 953.70 eV in pure Cu(II) to 932.51/ 952.33 eV in Cu(II)-M1 complex (Fig. 10d). The coordination of Cu(II) ions, loss of hydrogen bonds, and associated breakdown of clusters resulted in variable shifts of C1s BEs in Cu(II)-M1 complex (Table S15). In Cu(II)-M1, coordination among Cu(II) and −CO N H2,−CO N H–, and –C O OCH2– led to significant loss of pre-existing hydrogen bonds (Table S16) and shifts of amide-I from 1667/ 1616 cm–1 in M1 to 1660/ 1586 cm–1 in Cu(II)-M1 [81]. Herein, the coordination of >CO with Cu(II) could produce mixture of square-planar, square-pyramidal, and octahedral complexes, realized from peaks at 508, 571, 673, and 772 cm–1 [12]. Compared to M1, the coordination driven loss of pre-existing hydrogen bonds among esters and amides deteriorated the thermostability of Cu(II)-M1 complex (Fig. 10i), confirmed via elevated O1s BEs of −C O NH−CH2– from 540.49 eV in M1 to 541.89 eV in Cu(II)-M1 complex (Table S15) [82]. The displacement of water coordinated in Cu(NO3)2 by N and O donors, such as −COOCH2–, −CONH–, and −CONH2, accelerated the decomposition of Cu(II)-M1 complex, leading to substantial mass loss at 200 oC (Fig. 10i). Moreover, in Cu(II)-M1 complex, the superficial deposition was visualized in photomicrograph, substantiated via intense Cu(II) peak in the EDX spectrum (Fig. 10k,m).

In Fe(III)-M2 complex, the strong coordination of O-donors, i.e., –C O OCH2– and −C O NH–, with Fe(III) were confirmed by significantly elevated O1s BEs from 532.03 eV in M2 to 532.71 eV in Fe(III)-M2 complex (Fig. 10). The electron rich –C O OCH2– and −C O NH– donated electron density on Fe(III) of Fe(III)-M2 complex. Thus, Fe2p3/2 BE lowered significantly from 726.50 eV in FeCl3 to 723.64 eV in Fe(III)-M2 complex (Fig. 10h). The selective Fe(III) binding with –C O NH– appreciably altered broad amide-I peaks from 1655 and 1616 cm–1 in M2 to 1631 cm–1 in Fe(III)-M2 complex, along with the marginal change in amide-II peak from 1451 to 1447 cm–1 (Fig. S23). The Fe(III) coordination caused the breakdown of higher aggregates in Fe(III)-M2. Thus, the decrease in electron density of O-donors was consistent with elevated satellite O1s BEs of −C O NHCH2– from 541.47 eV in M2 to 541.78 eV in Fe(III)-M2 complex (Table S15). In Fe(III)-M2 complex, the massive disappearance of hydrogen bonds was confirmed by transformation of a broad peak at 3428 cm–1 to appreciably weakened bands at 3399 and 3316 cm–1. The new peak at 574 cm−1 could be correlated to Fe–O str. of Fe(III)-M2 complex. Compared to O-donors, weaker binding of Fe(III) with N-donors, i.e., −CO N H2 and −CO N H–, was confirmed by marginal shift of N1s BEs from 399.42/ 399.91 eV in M2 to 399.38/ 399.94 eV in Fe(III)-M2 complex, respectively (Fig. 3d and 10e). Moreover, >C=O coordination of Fe(III) appreciably lowered the stability of Fe(III)-M2 complex (Table S16). The rapid degradation of Fe(III)-M2 complex up to 220 oC was attributed to substantial loss of intra-/ inter-molecular hydrogen bonding because of Fe(III) coordination with >CO of amides, followed by restricted mass loss above 220 oC conferred by thermostable Fe(III)-complex (Fig. 10j). Herein, possible displacement of water coordinated by N and O donors accelerated the decomposition of Fe(III)-M2 complex, leading to significant mass loss at 200 oC (Fig. 10j).

In scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of Fe(III)-M2 complex, superficial deposition was visualized, confirmed via intense Fe(III) peak in EDX spectrum (Fig. 10l,n). Thus, Fe(III)-coordination associated fluorescence quenching was related to breakdown of hydrogen bonded aggregates and scarcity of nonbonding orbitals of carbonyl oxygen involved in coordinate bonding with Fe(III).

Here, Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 were desorbed at pH = 2.0. Then, M1 and M2 were applied further for adsorption at pH = 7.0. The adsorption-desorption studies upto fifth cycle were executed continuously to confirm the reusabilities and the stabilities of M1 and M2 (Fig. 11 a,b). The reusabilities of M1/ M2 were supported by > 91 % adsorption after the fifth cycle.

Moreover, the structural integrities were confirmed by measuring the intensity average hydrodynamic diameters of M1 and M2 in DMSO, i.e., D h, and M1 and M2 desorbed after sixth cycle, i.e., D hʹ. However, the closeness of D h, i.e., 146 and 129 nm, and D hʹ, i.e., 144 and 122 nm, for M1 and M2, respectively, confirmed retention of M1 and M2 networks (Fig. 11c,d). The dispersions of M1 and M2 were employed as invisible inks to write GCELT and SECURITY on TLC-plate. These words were clearly readable under UV-light at 310 nm (Fig. S24) [83].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging and photo/ storage stability
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability assays were measured using 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, and 1.2 μg μL–1 M1/ M2 to present biocompatibilities of M1 and M2 on normal Madin-Darby canine kidney (MDCK) cells (Fig. 12 a and b). From Fig. 12a,b, the non-cytotoxicities of M1 and M2 were substantiated via 94 and 92 % cell viabilities for M1 and M2, respectively, even at 1.2 μg μL–1 M1/ M2 (Fig. 12c–h). Compared to M1, the lower proliferation rate of MDCK cells in M2 resulted in the lower cell viabilities. Moreover, non-cytotoxic M1 and M2 used for imaging of MDCK cells through 519 nm wavelength filter after 20, 40, and 80 min incubation at 0.1 μg μL–1 M1/ M2 (Fig. 12c–h). However, the higher fluorescence intensities within the cells after 80 min incubation substantiated both cell and nuclear membrane permeabilities of M1 and M2 [13]. Additionally, time-dependent alterations of autofluorescence intensities were measured to support intracellular photo/ storage stabilities of M1 and M2 within MDCK cells (Fig. 12j,k). Here, time-dependent fluorescence intensities loss of M1/ M2 within MDCK cells (Fig. 12i,l) well fitted with second order decay equation, as supported from high adj. R 2, i.e., 0.9968 and 0.9976 for M1 and M2, respectively, and F-values, i.e., 16112.42 and 22278.44 for M1 and M2, respectively. For M1 and M2, average lifetimes of photobleaching were 100 and 75 s respectively, presenting high photo/ storage stability within MDCK cells due to variable extents of interactions of cell components with M1 and M2 [83].

The scalable aliphatic light-emitting M1 and M2 envisaging appreciably elevated AEEs have been designed, and prepared by CC and NC coupled surfactant free facile polymerization of non-fluorescent hydrophobic monomers in aqueous medium. The in situ anchored acrylamido-ester third comonomers have been confirmed by secondary amide peaks in XPS, NMR, and FTIR, supported from TDDFT and NTO analyses. The in situ formed secondary amides have enhanced rigidity in aggregates and AEEs, confirmed through DLS, HRTEM, and photophysical studies. The fluorescence of M1 and M2 have been closely related to alterations of geometry, bond angles, and bond lengths; smaller HL in S1 compared to those of S0; and MOs. Moreover, larger Δr and smaller σ he have inferred n–π* through space emissive charge transfers. M1 and M2 have envisaged excellent sensitivity towards rapid sensing of Cu(II) and Fe(III) at lower LODs in DMSO + water and pure water by coordination of N and/ or O donor(s) with Cu(II) and Fe(III), respectively, leading to rupture of hydrogen bonding and n–π* interaction driven aggregations. The selective coordination of O/ N donor(s), i.e., −CO N H2, −CO N H–, and –C O OCH2–, with Cu(II) and O donors, i.e., –C O OCH2– and −C O NH–, with Fe(III) in Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes, respectively, and turn-off fluorescence sensing have been confirmed by XPS, FTIR, TG, and SEM analyses of metal ions loaded microstructures, substantiated through TDDFT and NTO analyses of Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes. Fe(III) has envisaged the stronger coordination with O donors of M2. In contrast, Cu(II) presented strong coordination with both N and O donors of M1. Thus, the better overall coordination of Cu(II) with both N and O donors results in higher maximum adsorption capacity and sensitivity of M1 than M2 at optimized conditions, i.e., pH = 7.0 and adsorbent dose = 0.01 g. Nevertheless, these AEEgens are suitable for detections and removals of Cu(II) and Fe(III), cell imaging, and invisible inks. This approach of synthesis of nonaromatic macromolecular AEEgens by in situ anchored acrylamido-ester monomers in facile polymerization of hydrophobic monomers should be broadened for synthesizing other aliphatic nonaromatic fluorescent AEEgens for diverse applications.

Chloroform, dimethyl sulfoxide, and methanol of HPLC grade were procured from Sigma Aldrich. HCl, NaOH, PE, BP, EGM, NNBIS, PP, SB, silver nitrate, potassium chloride, copper(II) nitrate, chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate, iron(II) chloride, iron(III) chloride, nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, cadmium chloride, mercuric chloride, lead nitrate, antimony trichloride, bismuth(III) nitrate, and cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate of AR grades were obtained from Merck.
The AEEgens were characterized using techniques furnished in Table S17.

In aqueous medium, Cu(II)/ Fe(III) solutions up to 1000 mg L–1/ ppm were prepared. Thereafter, 0.01 g M1 and M2 were added to 50 mL Cu(II)/ Fe(III). The adsorption process was monitored by atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS). The concentrations of Cu(II) and Fe(III)/ C t were calculated from two calibrated equations. The q t values were determined from a previously reported method [12,50].

Herein, geometry optimization of M1 and M2 were performed at B3LYP/6−31+G(d) of DFT by using the Gaussian16 package. LANL2DZ/ 6−31+G(d) basis set was used for iron and copper/ other atoms. B3LYP-D3 with LANL2DZ optimized aggregated M1 and M2 alongside Cu(II)-M1 and Fe(III)-M2 complexes [84]. Frequency calculations of Cu(II)-M1/ Fe(III)-M2 complex and M1/ M2 by B3LYP/LANL2DZ and B3LYP/6−31+G(d) basis sets, respectively, presented optimized minima on energy surfaces. Absorption spectra were explored with TDDFT at PBEPBE/6−31 G(d, N = 30). The excited state optimizations and vertical excited energies were done at PBEPBE/6−31 G(d). Emission energies were measured from excited state (lowest) optimized geometries. The absorption and fluorescence spectra in DMSO were investigated using IEF-PCM. The GaussSum3.0 explored major transitions in spectra and orbital contributions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of LCST and average lifetime
ce:section.ce:para: LCST and average lifetime of M1 and M2 were calculated from a reported method [53,83].

MDCK cells cultured initially using a reported method [13], were subsequently exposed to 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, and 1.2 μg μL–1 M1 and M2 for 24 h. Followed by incubation, cell viability was measured using MTT [13]. These assays were performed thrice independently (n = 3). In each experiment, three replicates of one condition were considered to determine the mean ± SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in vitro cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: MDCK cells were initially cultured in a 35 mm petri dish on sterile poly L-lysine coated coverslips, and allowed to grow at 37 °C in humidified incubator with 95:5 (v/v) air/CO2. These cells were added to 0.1 μg μL–1 M1 and M2, and incubated for 20, 40, and 80 min. The cells were then washed with PBS, and fixed with 0.1 mL 4% (v/v) p-formaldehyde/water. The cover slip was positioned on grease free slides. The sides containing cells were mounted on glass slides by glycerol. The emissions of M1/ M2 treated cells and control were measured at 519 nm/ DAPI channel.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Arnab Dutta received B.Sc. from University of Calcutta in 2013 and M.Sc. from West Bengal State University in 2016. His research interests include synthesis, characterization, and application of intrinsically fluorescent polymeric materials for adsorption, sensing, bioimaging, and device applications.

Manas Mahapatra completed B.Sc. and M.Sc. from University of Calcutta in 2013 and 2015, respectively. His research interests comprise multicomponent synthesis of valuable nontraditional multifunctional smart polymers for sensing, separation, security device, and energy applications.

Madhushree Mitra completed B.Sc. from University of Calcutta in 2012 and M.Sc. from Vidyasagar University in 2014. Her research interests include synthesis, characterisation, and application of intrinsically fluorescent multifunctional polymeric materials for adsorption, sensing, bioimaging, and device applications.

Akash Banerjee completed B.Sc. from West Bengal State University in 2016 and M.Sc. from University of Calcutta in 2018. His research interests include synthesis and characterisation of fluorescent polymers and MOFs for sensing, separation, and device applications.

Narendra Nath Ghosh completed B.Sc. and M.Sc. from University of North Bengal in 2007 and 2009, respectively. His research interests include understanding the fluorescence phenomena of nontraditional smart fluorescent polymers and the electronic structure of organic dyes-inorganic semiconductor nanohybrid materials for designing of efficient dye sensitized solar cell.

Dr. Pijush Kanti Chattopadhyay received Ph.D. from Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology (IIT), Kharagpur in 2012. He is working as Assistant Professor in the department of Leather Technology, Government College of Engineering and Leather Technology (Post Graduate), Maulana Abul Kalam Azad University of Technology, Kolkata, India. His field of interests include development of rubber nanocomposites, multipolymeric adsorbents, sensitive and selective purely aliphatic fluorescent sensors, and environmentally friendly collagen based composites for cost-friendly waste managements. Presently, he is working on the development of renewable components based multi-functional multipolymeric materials capable of sensitive and selective detection cum adsorptive removals of toxic pollutants from industrial effluents. Dr. Chattopadhyay published more than sixty (60) research items including articles, reviews, and book chapters.

Professor Dilip Kumar Maiti received Ph.D. from CSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Biology (IICB), Kolkata in 1998. He is currently a Professor in the department of Chemistry, University of Calcutta, Kolkata, India. Professor Maiti has been working on the synthesis of organic compounds through diverse C–H activation, photocatalysis, NHC-catalysis, organocatalysis, and dual catalysis. In addition, he is developing smart materials for sensing poisonous phosphate, hydrazine, cyanide; inkless writing; and fabricating memory devices. He has published more than hundred research articles in international journals of high repute.

Dr. Nayan Ranjan Singha obtained Ph.D. from University of Calcutta in 2010, and underwent postdoctoral studies at University of Calcutta (2011–2013) and Jadavpur University (2013–2014). Dr. Singha joined as Assistant Professor, and started independent research career at Government College of Engineering and Leather Technology (Post-Graduate), Maulana Abul Kalam Azad University of Technology, Kolkata, India. Dr. Singha has been working on strategic synthesis of intrinsically fluorescent light-emitting polymers, nonconventional conducting macromolecules, and multifunctional stimuli responsive hydrogels suitable for diverse applications, such as sensing, separation, bioimaging, device, and energy; fabrication of natural and/ or synthetic polymer(s) and waste collagenic material based multipolymer hybrid biocomposites; waste recycling/ management; monitoring of plant signalling molecules; synthetic polymer and graphene/ CNT based nanocomposites for sensing, separation, and device application(s); and computational chemistry. Dr. Singha corresponded/ published more than sixty (60) research items including articles, reviews, and book chapters.
